summary: Gruber dissects Steve Jobs's 'Thoughts on Music' essay, arguing it simultaneously challenges the major record labels to drop DRM, rebuffs European governments demanding Apple 'open up' FairPlay, and delivers a strategic blow to Microsoft. He highlights Jobs's framing of three options — status quo, licensing FairPlay, or dropping DRM — as a rhetorical move that eliminates the middle option, leaving only the status quo or DRM-free music. Gruber notes the tension between Jobs's anti-DRM rhetoric and Apple's refusal to let indie labels sell DRM-free music on iTunes, calling it a test of sincerity. He also observes that the music industry's response reveals fundamental confusion, with the RIAA bizarrely celebrating an offer Jobs explicitly rejected, and Microsoft's Zune team cycling through the stages of grief in two paragraphs.
key_points: Jobs's essay is strategically designed to serve multiple purposes: challenging labels, answering European regulators, and undermining Microsoft's position
DRM and interoperability are mutually exclusive — 'open DRM' is a contradiction, and the only real path to interoperability is no DRM at all
Apple's sincerity on DRM-free music would be proven by allowing indie labels who already want it to sell unprotected tracks on iTunes
The music industry's insistence on DRM is hypocritical given that 90% of music is already sold DRM-free on CDs
Eliminating DRM would also kill subscription-based music services, which is a competitive side benefit for Apple
money_quotes: If it's a bluff, all it will take to call them on it is a simple two-character statement from just one of the four major labels: 'OK'.
If you really want interoperability, then what you want is no DRM, not 'open' DRM.
Translation: We are not going to license FairPlay to anyone else. The only options that are actually on the table are the status quo and dropping DRM.
News flash, Mr. Anonymous: CDs are digital, unprotected, and higher-quality than the compressed format on iTunes.
In other words, the music industry wants a magical DRM format that gives them — not Apple, not Microsoft — complete control over all digital music. And a unicorn and a rainbow.
Apple is not Microsoft; the only competitive advantage Apple needs is the quality of its products.
The only thing the Zune team is doing 'on the ground with the industry' is squirting each other.
key_insight: Jobs's essay is a masterful strategic move that frames the DRM debate so that the only viable options are the status quo or DRM-free music, putting the onus squarely on the record labels while deflecting regulatory pressure from Apple.
